Client 2: And I’m Michael. I want the math to be right—income sources, taxes, and what we can actually spend.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it plain English and accurate. Let’s start with the big picture: across the household, what’s your gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: I think it’s around one-thirty-ish, but the exact household gross income is 129700 a year.
Advisor: Got it—129700 gross annually. And that’s combining what’s coming in from both of you, Patricia and Michael.
Advisor: Next, on the spending side—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, food, travel, everything.

Advisor: Got it—129700 gross annually. And that’s combining what’s coming in from both of you, Patricia and Michael.
Advisor: Next, on the spending side—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, food, travel, everything.
Client 2: We track it pretty closely. Total monthly expenses are 5500 a month.
Advisor: Okay, 5500 per month. One sec—just noting that down.
Advisor: And then debt payments—so minimum payments on credit cards, car loans, student loans, anything like that. What’s the total monthly debt payments number?

Client 2: And I’m Michael. I want the math to be right—income sources, taxes, and what we can actually spend.

Advisor: Great—so no ongoing debt payments.
Advisor: Let’s shift to what you’ve built up. Roughly speaking, what’s the total you’d consider investable assets—things like brokerage accounts, retirement accounts, cash that’s earmarked for investing?
Client 2: Total investable assets are 694300.
Advisor: Thanks. And we’ll break that into the individual accounts in a minute so it matches your statements.
Advisor: Before we do, just to confirm—no loans outstanding at all? No mortgage, no HELOC, nothing like that?

Advisor: Perfect, thanks for the correction.
Advisor: And then your home—just so we have the full net worth picture—even though it’s not investable in the same way. What are you using as the current value for your primary residence?
Client 2: Primary residence value is 258950.
Advisor: Okay—258950 for the home. And you said no mortgage, so that’s clean equity.
Advisor: Last area: protection. Patricia, do you have long-term care coverage in place?

Client 1: We’re using 258950 for the property value.
Advisor: Thanks. One sec—just noting that down.
Advisor: So, big picture: you’ve got the rental income and the quarterly interest and dividends on the income side, the joint taxable and retirement and cash and the private markets on the asset side, the home at 258950, and then no debts—loans outstanding 0, mortgage 0, non-mortgage 0. And Patricia, the long-term care policy is in place. Did I miss anything material?
Client 1: No, that’s it—nothing else material.
Advisor: Okay, thanks—super helpful. I want to zoom out for a couple household basics that drive the plan. First, just to confirm: Patricia and Michael, are you married?

Advisor: Okay. Next one is more about how we work together. Are you looking for an ongoing relationship where we’re acting as your RIA (registered investment advisor), or are you thinking more one-time planning?
Client 1: Ongoing. RIA (registered investment advisor) is what we want.
Advisor: Perfect. And then risk—when markets get choppy, how do you want the portfolio positioned? More conservative, balanced, or growth?
Client 1: I’d say growth—like, I can tolerate swings if we’ve got the cash buffer.
Advisor: Okay—growth. Michael, same page, or do you see it differently?

Advisor: So, big picture: you’ve got the rental income and the quarterly interest and dividends on the income side, the joint taxable and retirement and cash and the private markets on the asset side, the home at 258950, and then no debts—loans outstanding 0, mortgage 0, non-mortgage 0. And Patricia, the long-term care policy is in place. Did I miss anything material?
Client 1: No, that’s it—nothing else material.
Advisor: Okay, thanks—super helpful. I want to zoom out for a couple household basics that drive the plan. First, just to confirm: Patricia and Michael, are you married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And dependents—anyone financially dependent on you right now? Kids still on the payroll, parents you support, anything like that?

Client 1: Yes, 2 adults.
Advisor: Great. Last one on this admin-y set: what tax bracket are you generally landing in these days—ballpark is fine.
Client 1: I always mix this up—like, I think we’re in the 35% bracket.
Advisor: Got it—35%. Michael, does that match what you’re seeing when you run the numbers?
Client 2: Yes, 35% is right for us.

Advisor: Hi Patricia, hi Michael—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Patricia. We’re trying to get a clear retirement timeline and just… make sure we’re not missing anything big.
Client 2: And I’m Michael. I want the math to be right—income sources, taxes, and what we can actually spend.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it plain English and accurate. Let’s start with the big picture: across the household, what’s your gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: I think it’s around one-thirty-ish, but the exact household gross income is 129700 a year.

Client 1: Yes, joint at the bank. The value is 38250.
Advisor: Thanks.
Advisor: Any other investments that are a bit less traditional—private investments or private markets—held at a bank?
Client 2: That one’s Patricia’s, actually.
Client 1: Right—mine. Private markets held at the bank, value is 32450.

Advisor: Hi Patricia, hi Michael—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?

Advisor: Okay, let’s walk through income sources briefly so I understand what makes up that 129700. Patricia, do you have any earned income right now, or is it more passive income?
Client 1: I’m not working right now—unemployed. My gross income is 103250 annually, mostly from our rental.
Advisor: Understood. Michael, how about you—working, retired, or something in between?
Client 2: I’m inactive—no job income. My gross income is 26450 annually.

Advisor: Patricia, Michael—before you go, I realized I didn’t pin down something basic on the income side for each of you. Patricia, what would you call your current employment status?
Client 1: I’m unemployed right now—so, unemployed.

Client 1: Business owner. I still think of myself as a business owner even though I’m not working day-to-day.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I label things correctly in the plan—Patricia, you’re the primary in the household, right?

Advisor: Good. That simplifies things.
